---
title: "STA 506 Final Examination"
author: "Sabah Khan"
date: " Due: May 5, 2026 "
output:
  html_document: 
    toc: yes
    toc_depth: 4
    toc_float: yes
    number_sections: no
    toc_collapsed: yes
    code_folding: hide
    code_download: yes
    smooth_scroll: yes
    highlight: monochrome
    theme: spacelab
  pdf_document: 
    toc: yes
    toc_depth: 4
    fig_caption: yes
    number_sections: no
    fig_width: 3
    fig_height: 3
  word_document: 
    toc: yes
    toc_depth: 4
    fig_caption: yes
    keep_md: yes
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: inline
---

```{css, echo = FALSE}
#TOC::before {
  content: "Table of Contents";
  font-weight: bold;
  font-size: 1.2em;
  display: block;
  color: navy;
  margin-bottom: 10px;
}


div#TOC li {     /* table of content  */
    list-style:upper-roman;
    background-image:none;
    background-repeat:none;
    background-position:0;
}

h1.title {    /* level 1 header of title  */
  font-size: 22px;
  font-weight: bold;
  color: DarkRed;
  text-align: center;
  font-family: "Gill Sans", sans-serif;
}

h4.author { /* Header 4 - and the author and data headers use this too  */
  font-size: 15px;
  font-weight: bold;
  font-family: system-ui;
  color: navy;
  text-align: center;
}

h4.date { /* Header 4 - and the author and data headers use this too  */
  font-size: 18px;
  font-weight: bold;
  font-family: "Gill Sans", sans-serif;
  color: DarkBlue;
  text-align: center;
}

h1 { /* Header 1 - and the author and data headers use this too  */
    font-size: 20px;
    font-weight: bold;
    font-family: "Times New Roman", Times, serif;
    color: darkred;
    text-align: center;
}

h2 { /* Header 2 - and the author and data headers use this too  */
    font-size: 18px;
    font-weight: bold;
    font-family: "Times New Roman", Times, serif;
    color: navy;
    text-align: left;
}

h3 { /* Header 3 - and the author and data headers use this too  */
    font-size: 16px;
    font-weight: bold;
    font-family: "Times New Roman", Times, serif;
    color: navy;
    text-align: left;
}

h4 { /* Header 4 - and the author and data headers use this too  */
    font-size: 14px;
  font-weight: bold;
    font-family: "Times New Roman", Times, serif;
    color: darkred;
    text-align: left;
}

/* Add dots after numbered headers */
.header-section-number::after {
  content: ".";

body { background-color:white; }

.highlightme { background-color:yellow; }

p { background-color:white; }

}
```

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
# code chunk specifies whether the R code, warnings, and output 
# will be included in the output files.
if (!require("knitr")) {
   install.packages("knitr")
   library(knitr)
}
if (!require("pander")) {
   install.packages("pander")
   library(pander)
}
if (!require("ggplot2")) {
  install.packages("ggplot2")
  library(ggplot2)
}
if (!require("tidyverse")) {
  install.packages("tidyverse")
  library(tidyverse)
}
if (!require("boot")) {
  install.packages("boot")
  library(boot)
}
if (!require("plotly")) {
  install.packages("plotly")
  library(plotly)
}
####
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE,       # include code chunk in the output file
                      warning = FALSE,   # sometimes, you code may produce warning messages,
                                         # you can choose to include the warning messages in
                                         # the output file. 
                      results = TRUE,    # you can also decide whether to include the output
                                         # in the output file.
                      message = FALSE,
                      comment = NA
                      )  
```
 
 \

## Part A: Methodological Derivations

\

### **Problem A1**: 
Show that the density function of the Kumaraswamy distribution is

$$
f(x; a, b) = ab \, x^{a-1} (1 - x^a)^{b-1}.
$$
The PDF is the derivative of the CDF:

$$
f(x;a,b)= d/dx, F(x;a,b) = d/dx,  [1âˆ’(1âˆ’x^a) ^b] 
$$
The derivative of the constant 1 is 0, so:
$$
f(x;a,b)=âˆ’d/dx(1âˆ’x^a) ^b
$$
Applying the chain rule:

= âˆ’b(1âˆ’x^a) ^ (bâˆ’1) (d/dx(1âˆ’x^a)) = 

d/dx (1âˆ’x^a)= âˆ’ax ^(aâˆ’1)

Substituting back:
f(x;a,b) = âˆ’b (1âˆ’x^a) ^ (bâˆ’1) (âˆ’ax ^ (aâˆ’1))

The two negatives cancel:

f(x;a,b) = [(ab) x^ (aâˆ’1)] [(1âˆ’x ^a) ^ (bâˆ’1)]

\

### **Problem A2**: 
Let $\{x_1, x_2, \cdots, x_n \}$ be an i.i.d. random sample taken from a population that follows the aove 2-parameter Kumaraswamy distribution. Write out the loglikelihood function of $a$ and $b$, denoted by $\ell(a,b)$, based on the above random sample and **derive** the gradient vector $[\ell_a^\prime(a,b), \ell_b^\prime(a,b)]$, the first order partial derivative of the log-likelihood with respect to parameters $a$ and $b$.

Take the natural log of the pdf:

â„“(a,b)=i=1âˆ‘nâ€‹log[abx^(aâˆ’1) (1âˆ’x^a) ^ (bâˆ’1)]

Expanding using log properties:

â„“(a,b)=nloga+nlogb+(aâˆ’1)i=1âˆ‘n logx +(bâˆ’1)i=1âˆ‘n log(1âˆ’(x^a))

Partial derivative with respect to b:

=(n/b) + i=1âˆ‘n log(1âˆ’(x^a))

Partial derivative with respect to a:

=(n/a) + i=1âˆ‘n logx +(bâˆ’1) i=1âˆ‘n âˆ‚/âˆ‚a log(1âˆ’(x^a))

Applying the chain rule to the last term:

âˆ‚/âˆ‚a log(1âˆ’(x^a)) = (-x^a(log x))/(1âˆ’(x^a))

Substituting back:

n/a + i=1âˆ‘n logx - (bâˆ’1) i=1âˆ‘n (x^a(log x))/(1âˆ’(x^a))

\

### **Problem A3**: 
Based on the gradients functions obtained in the above problem A2, **derive** the observed Fisher Information matrix (i.e, the negative Hessian Matrix).

The Observed Fisher Information Matrix is the negative Hessian:

-[â„“aaâ€²â€²â€‹â„“abâ€²â€²
  â„“baâ€²â€²â€‹â„“bbâ€²â€²]

The gradients derived in A2 were:

for b: (n/b) + i=1âˆ‘n log(1âˆ’(x^a))

for a: (n/a) + i=1âˆ‘n logx - (bâˆ’1) i=1âˆ‘n (x^a(log x))/(1âˆ’(x^a))

â„“bbâ€²â€² = âˆ‚/âˆ‚b [(n/b) + i=1âˆ‘n log(1âˆ’(x^a))] = -n/b^2

differentiate â„“bâ€² with respect to a:

â„“abâ€²â€² = âˆ‚/âˆ‚a [(n/b) + i=1âˆ‘n log(1âˆ’(x^a)) ] = i=1âˆ‘n (-x^a(log x))/(1âˆ’(x^a))

differentiate â„“aâ€² with respect to a:

â„“aaâ€²â€² = âˆ‚/âˆ‚a [(n/a) + logx - (bâˆ’1) i=1âˆ‘n (x^a(log x))/(1âˆ’(x^a))]

The first two terms give âˆ’n/a^2. For the last term, the quotient rule was applied:

âˆ‚/âˆ‚a (x^a(log x)) / (1âˆ’(x^a)) = [(1âˆ’(x^a) (x^a)(logx) ^2)-[(x^a(log x))(-x^a(log x))]]/((1âˆ’(x^a) ^2)

= [(x^a)(logx) ^2) (1âˆ’(x^a) + ((x^2a)(logx) ^2)]/ ((1âˆ’(x^a) ^2)

â„“aaâ€²â€² = -n/a^2 - (b-1) i=1âˆ‘n [(x^a)(logx) ^2) / ((1âˆ’(x^a) ^2)]

So the matrix is 


-[â„“aaâ€²â€²â€‹â„“abâ€²â€²
  â„“baâ€²â€²â€‹â„“bbâ€²â€²]
  
  =
  
[(n/a^2) + (b-1) i=1âˆ‘n [(x^a)(logx) ^2) / ((1âˆ’(x^a) ^2)] ,  i=1âˆ‘n (x^a)(log x)))/1âˆ’(x^a),
i=1âˆ‘n (x^a)(logx) / 1âˆ’(x^a), n/b^2 ]


\

### **Problem A4**: 

Consider power distribution $F(x) = x^a, (a >0 \quad \text{ and }\quad x \in (0,1))$, a special case of the Kumaraswamy distribution with $b = 1$, and a random sample from this distribution $\{ x_1, x_2, \cdots, x_n\}$. **Derive** the MLE and MME of $a$ respectively. [*Hint: To find the MME, you need to compute the moment of the power distribution; that is, $E[X^k] = \int_0^1 x^k F'(x) dx$. Note that both the MLE and the MME have closed-form expressions.*]

When b=1, the Kumaraswamy PDF is:

f(x;a)=ax^(a-1), xâˆˆ (0,1), a>0 

The log-likelihood from A2 with b=1 is:

â„“(a)=nloga+(aâˆ’1) i=1âˆ‘n logx + 0

since (bâˆ’1) âˆ‘log(1-x^a) = 0 when b=1.

Taking the derivative and setting equal to zero:

â„“â€²(a)= (n/a) + i=1âˆ‘n logx = 0

solving for a:

n/a = - i=1âˆ‘n logx

MLE of a = -n/(i=1âˆ‘n logx)


E[X^k]=âˆ« x^k Fâ€²(x)dx (integral is from zero to one)

E[X]=âˆ«xax^(a-1)dx (integral is from zero to one) = aâˆ«x^(a)dx= (integral is from zero to one)

= a (x^(a+1)/a+1) from zero to one, = a/a+1

Method of Moments (MME), E[X] = mean x

a/a+1 = mean x

a = mean x(a+1) = mean x(a) + (mean x)

= a - mean x(a) = mean x

a(1-mean x) = mean x

MME for a = [(mean x)/(1-mean x)]
\

### **Problem A5**:

Using the same setting as in **Problem A4**, find the asymptotic (Wald) confidence interval for $a$. [*Hint: Compute the Fisher information for $a$, then take its reciprocal to obtain the variance*.]

With b=1, the log-likelihood is:

â„“(a)=nloga+(aâˆ’1) i=1âˆ‘n logx

The second derivative is:

â„“â€²â€²(a)= -n/(a^2)

so the Fisher information is âˆ’â„“â€²â€²(a) = n/(a^2)

The hint says that the variance is the reciprocal of the Fisher information so the variance = (a^2)/n

and the standard deviation is a/sqrt n

The Wald Confidence interval is a Â± z Î±/2(sd(a) where z Î±/2 is the upper critical value of the standard normal

\

### **Problem A6**:

Using the same setting as in **Problem A4**, perform a likelihood ratio test for the hypothesis $H_0 :a=1$ (i.e., the power distribution reduces to a uniform distribution). [*Hint: Evaluate the log-likelihood function at the maximum likelihood estimate $\hat{a}$ and at $a=1$, then use these values to construct the LRT test statistic.*]

From A2 with b=1:

â„“(a)=nloga+(aâˆ’1) i=1âˆ‘n logx

Log-likelihood at the MLE a-hat:

subsituting a-hat = -n/(i=1âˆ‘n logx). Let T = i=1âˆ‘n logx

â„“(a-hat)=nlog(-n/T)+((-n/T) - 1)T

=nlog(-n/T)-n-T

Log-likelihood under H0:a=1:

â„“(1)=nlog(1)+(1âˆ’1)i=1âˆ‘n logx = 0

LRT Statistic By Wilks' theorem:

Î›=2[â„“(a^)âˆ’â„“(1)]=2[nlog(-n/T)-n-T-0]

Î›=2[nlog(-n/(i=1âˆ‘n logx))-n- i=1âˆ‘n logx]

under H0 with p-value =P(Ï‡12>Î›) where Ï‡12 is chi-square with 1 degree of freedom

\

## Part B: Numerical Analysis

**Working Dataset**: A small reservoir supplies water to a town. During the dry season (50 days), engineers record the fraction of usable storage filled each morning. Values near 0 mean the reservoir is nearly empty; values near 1 mean it's full. The distribution tends to be rightâ€‘skewed (mostly low levels due to drought) but with occasional replenishment.

The following 50 data points (ordered for clarity) represent the daily proportion of usable storage:

```
0.12, 0.14, 0.15, 0.16, 0.17, 0.18, 0.19, 0.20, 0.21, 0.22,
0.23, 0.24, 0.25, 0.26, 0.27, 0.28, 0.29, 0.30, 0.31, 0.32,
0.33, 0.34, 0.35, 0.36, 0.37, 0.38, 0.39, 0.40, 0.41, 0.42,
0.43, 0.44, 0.45, 0.46, 0.47, 0.48, 0.49, 0.50, 0.51, 0.52,
0.53, 0.54, 0.55, 0.56, 0.57, 0.58, 0.59, 0.60, 0.61, 0.78
```

\

### **Problem B1**:

Fit the Kumaraswamy distribution to the above data. Use the derivations in **Problem A2** to find the MLE of $a$ and $b$. Please copy the key formulas before coding.

```{r}

reservoir <- c(
  0.12, 0.14, 0.15, 0.16, 0.17, 0.18, 0.19, 0.20, 0.21, 0.22,
  0.23, 0.24, 0.25, 0.26, 0.27, 0.28, 0.29, 0.30, 0.31, 0.32,
  0.33, 0.34, 0.35, 0.36, 0.37, 0.38, 0.39, 0.40, 0.41, 0.42,
  0.43, 0.44, 0.45, 0.46, 0.47, 0.48, 0.49, 0.50, 0.51, 0.52,
  0.53, 0.54, 0.55, 0.56, 0.57, 0.58, 0.59, 0.60, 0.61, 0.78
)

n <- length(reservoir)

# Log-likelihood from A2
# l(a,b) = n*log(a) + n*log(b) + (a-1)*sum(log(x)) + (b-1)*sum(log(1 - x^a))

kuma.loglik <- function(params, data) {
  a <- params[1]
  b <- params[2]
  n <- length(data)
  
#return -Inf for invalid parameter values
  if (a <= 0 || b <= 0) return(-Inf)
  
#x^a must be positive 
  
  inner <- 1 - data^a
  if (any(inner <= 0)) return(-Inf)

  loglik <- n * log(a) +
            n * log(b) +
            (a - 1) * sum(log(data)) +
            (b - 1) * sum(log(inner))

  return(loglik)
}

# Gradient vector from A2:

# dl/da = n/a + sum(log(x)) - (b-1)*sum(x^a*log(x)/(1-x^a))
# dl/db = n/b + sum(log(1 - x^a))

kuma.score <- function(params, data) {
  a <- params[1]
  b <- params[2]
  n <- length(data)

  xa      <- data^a                    # xi^a for all i
  log.x   <- log(data)                 # log(xi) for all i
  inner   <- 1 - xa                    # 1 - xi^a for all i
  
  # Partial derivative w.r.t. a
  grad_a <- n / a +
            sum(log.x) -
            (b - 1) * sum(xa * log.x / inner)
  
  # Partial derivative w.r.t. b
  grad_b <- n / b +
            sum(log(inner))

  return(c(grad_a, grad_b))
}

# Initial Values

# Using MME of power distribution for a from A4: a = mean x/(1-mean x)
# Using b = 1 as starting point

x_bar       <- mean(reservoir)
a_init      <- x_bar / (1 - x_bar)    # MME estimate for a
b_init      <- 1                       # start for b
init.params <- c(a = a_init, b = b_init)

cat("Initial values -> a:", round(a_init, 4), "  b:", round(b_init, 4), "\n")

# Running optim with gradient

mle.result <- optim(
  par     = init.params,
  fn      = kuma.loglik,
  gr      = kuma.score,
  data    = reservoir,
  method  = "L-BFGS-B",               
  lower   = c(1e-6, 1e-6),            #a > 0, b > 0
  hessian = TRUE,                      
  control = list(
    fnscale = -1,                      
    maxit   = 1000,
    abstol  = 1e-8,
    trace   = FALSE
  )
)


Hess  <- mle.result$hessian
covar <- solve(-Hess)                  # invert negative Hessian


a_hat <- mle.result$par["a"]
b_hat <- mle.result$par["b"]
se_a  <- sqrt(covar[1, 1])            # standard error of a_hat
se_b  <- sqrt(covar[2, 2])            # standard error of b_hat

cat("\n MLE Results \n",
"a_hat:", round(a_hat, 4), "  SE:", round(se_a, 4), "\n",
"b_hat:", round(b_hat, 4), "  SE:", round(se_b, 4), "\n")



```

\

### **Problem B2**:

Fit the **power distribution** to the above data using the derived  of $a$ obtained in **Problem A4** to test the following hypothesis using likelihood ratio procedure ar significance level $\alpha = 0.05$:

$$
H_0: b = 1 \quad \text{ versus } \quad H_a: b \ne 1.
$$

State the statistical decision clearly. What is the practical implication of the testing result?

```{r}


# Restricted MLE under H0: b = 1 (power distribution)
# Closed-form MLE from A4: a_hat = -n / sum(log(x))
# Restricted MLE of a (closed-form from A4)

# MLE of Power distribution (Kumaraswamy with b = 1)

power.mle <- function(data) {
  n <- length(data)
  a_hat <- -n / sum(log(data))
  return(a_hat)
}

a_hat <- power.mle(reservoir)        
cat("H0 (b = 1) \n", "a_hat (MLE under H0):", round(a_hat, 4), "\n")


# Restricted log-likelihood at (a_hat, b = 1) 
# From A6: l(a, b=1) = n*log(a) + (a-1)*sum(log(x))
# the (b-1)*sum(log(1 - x^a)) term vanishes when b = 1

ll_restricted <- kuma.loglik(c(a_hat, 1), reservoir)
cat("Log-likelihood at (a_hat, b=1):", round(ll_restricted, 4), "\n")


# Unrestricted MLE of (a, b) from B1

x_bar       <- mean(reservoir)
a_init      <- x_bar / (1 - x_bar)    # MME initial value for a
b_init      <- 1                       # start for b

mle.result <- optim(
  par     = c(a = a_init, b = b_init),
  fn      = kuma.loglik,
  gr      = kuma.score,
  data    = reservoir,
  method  = "L-BFGS-B",
  lower   = c(1e-6, 1e-6),            # a > 0, b > 0
  hessian = TRUE,
  control = list(
    fnscale = -1,                      
    maxit   = 1000,
    abstol  = 1e-8,
    trace   = FALSE
  )
)

# Unrestricted MLEs and log-likelihood 
a_hat <- mle.result$par["a"]
b_hat <- mle.result$par["b"]
ll_unrestricted <- mle.result$value   # maximized log-likelihood

cat("\n Unrestricted MLE (Kumaraswamy) \n", "a_hat:", round(a_hat, 4), "b_hat:", round(b_hat, 4), "\n",


"Log-likelihood at (a_hat, b_hat):", round(ll_unrestricted, 4), "\n")


# LRT Statistic

# Lambda = 2 * [l(a_hat, b_hat) - l(a_hat, 1)] ~ chi^2(1)

alpha     <- 0.05               # significance level
df        <- 1                  # 1 degree of freedom

LRT <- 2 * (ll_unrestricted - ll_restricted)

# Critical value and p-value
crit.val  <- qchisq(1 - alpha, df = df)
p.val     <- 1 - pchisq(LRT, df = df)



cat("\n Likelihood Ratio Test: H0: b = 1 vs Ha: b does not = 1 \n",
"LRT statistic:          ", round(LRT, 4),     "\n",
"Critical value (df=1):  ", round(crit.val, 4),"\n",
"p-value:                ", round(p.val, 4),   "\n")


```
Reject the null hypothesis (that b=1. This does not fit a power distribution). 

\

### **Problem B3**:

Use the procedure and code from **Problem B1** to estimate the MLEs of $a$ and $b$, and then complete the following analyses:

(1). Obtain the bootstrap sampling distributions of $\hat{a}$ and $\hat{b}$ and plot each distribution using **Gaussian kernel density curves**.

(2).  Construct both the $95\%$ **bootstrap confidence interval** and the **Wald confidence interval** for $b$. Do these intervals agree with the results obtained in **Problem B2**? [*Compute the standard error of $\hat{b}$ using the observed Fisher information matrix, i.e., the inverse of the negative Hessian obtained from optim()*]

(3). Based on the bootstrap sampling distributions from part (1) of this problem, assess whether the validity of the Wald confidence interval is supported.

```{r}

# Initial values: MME for a, neutral start for b
x_bar       <- mean(reservoir)
a_init      <- x_bar / (1 - x_bar)
b_init      <- 1

# Run optim to get MLEs 
mle.result <- optim(
  par     = c(a = a_init, b = b_init),
  fn      = kuma.loglik,
  gr      = kuma.score,
  data    = reservoir,
  method  = "L-BFGS-B",
  lower   = c(1e-6, 1e-6),
  hessian = TRUE,
  control = list(fnscale = -1, maxit = 1000, abstol = 1e-8, trace = FALSE)
)

a_hat <- mle.result$par["a"]     # unrestricted MLE of a
b_hat <- mle.result$par["b"]     # unrestricted MLE of b

cat(" Original MLEs \n",
"a_hat:", round(a_hat, 4), "\n",
"b_hat:", round(b_hat, 4), "\n")

#Wald CI for b using observed Fisher Information

# Covariance matrix from negative Hessian 
Hess     <- mle.result$hessian         # Hessian from optim
covar    <- solve(-Hess)               # invert negative Hessian for covariance matrix
se_b     <- sqrt(covar[2, 2])         # SE of b_hat

# 95% Wald CI for b
z_crit      <- qnorm(0.975)            # 1.96 for 95% CI
wald_ci_b   <- c(
  lower = b_hat - z_crit * se_b,
  upper = b_hat + z_crit * se_b
)

cat("\n Wald CI for b \n",
"SE of b_hat:", round(se_b, 4), "\n",
"95% Wald CI: (", round(wald_ci_b["lower.b"], 4),
    ",", round(wald_ci_b["upper.b"], 4), ")\n")

```

These intervals agree with B2 because B2 confirmed that b does not equal 1. The Wald CI ranges from about 3 to about 12. 

```{r}

set.seed(123)  #
B <- 2000      # number of bootstrap replicates


# bootstrap sample
kuma.mle.boot <- function(data, indices) {
  boot.sample <- data[indices]  

  # Attempt MLE on bootstrap sample; return NA if optim did not converge
  fit <- optim(
    par     = c(a = x_bar / (1 - x_bar), b = 1),
    fn      = kuma.loglik,
    gr      = kuma.score,
    data    = boot.sample,
    method  = "L-BFGS-B",
    lower   = c(1e-6, 1e-6),
    control = list(fnscale = -1, maxit = 1000, abstol = 1e-8, trace = FALSE)
  )

  # optim() returns convergence = 0 on success, non-zero on failure
  if (fit$convergence == 0) {
    return(fit$par)          # return c(a_hat, b_hat)
  } else {
    return(c(NA, NA))        # return NA pair if optim() failed
  }
}

boot.result <- boot(
  data      = reservoir,
  statistic = kuma.mle.boot,
  R         = B
)

# Extract bootstrap distributions for a and b 
boot_a <- boot.result$t[, 1]          # bootstrap a_hat values
boot_b <- boot.result$t[, 2]          # bootstrap b_hat values


# bootstrap distributions with KDE
par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1))

# Bootstrap distribution of a_hat
plot(density(boot_a, kernel = "gaussian"),
     main = "Bootstrap Distribution of a_hat",
     xlab = "a_hat", ylab = "Density",
     lwd = 2, col = "blue")
abline(v = a_hat, col = "red", lwd = 2, lty = 2)   # original MLE
abline(v = mean(boot_a), col = "green", lwd = 2, lty = 3) # bootstrap mean
legend("topright",
       legend = c("KDE", "Original MLE", "Bootstrap Mean"),
       col    = c("blue", "red", "green"),
       lwd    = 2, lty = c(1, 2, 3), cex = 0.8)

# Bootstrap distribution of b_hat
plot(density(boot_b, kernel = "gaussian"),
     main = "Bootstrap Distribution of b_hat",
     xlab = "b_hat", ylab = "Density",
     lwd = 2, col = "orange")
abline(v = b_hat, col = "red", lwd = 2, lty = 2)   # original MLE
abline(v = mean(boot_b), col = "green", lwd = 2, lty = 3) # bootstrap mean
legend("topright",
       legend = c("KDE", "Original MLE", "Bootstrap Mean"),
       col    = c("orange", "red", "green"),
       lwd    = 2, lty = c(1, 2, 3, 4), cex = 0.8)

```

```{r}

alpha <- 0.05

# Percentile bootstrap CI for b 
boot_ci_b <- boot.ci(boot.result, conf = 1 - alpha,
                     type = "perc", index = 2)   # index=2 -> b

# Extract percentile CI bounds
perc_ci_b <- boot_ci_b$percent[4:5]   # [lower, upper]

cat("Bootstrap Percentile CI: (",
    round(perc_ci_b[1], 4), ",", round(perc_ci_b[2], 4), ")\n")


```

These intervals agree with B2 because B2 confirmed that b does not equal 1. The percentile CI ranges from about 5 to about 16. 
\

Based on the bootstrap sampling distributions, the validity of the Wald confidence interval is supported. The KDE graph shows a mean of around 7 which is what the original MLE was, and this point estimate also falls within the Wald CIs. Furthermore, looking at the graph, the CI values of 3 for upper and 12 for lower are plausible. 

### **Problem B4**:

In the introduction to the working model for this exam, the Kumaraswamy distribution reduces to the uniform distribution on (0,1). In this problem, we perform a **likelihood ratio test** for the following hypothesis to assess whether the data come from the uniform distribution on (0,1):

$$
H_0: a = 1\quad \& \quad b = 1\quad \text{ versus } \quad H_a: a \ne 1 \quad \text{or} \quad b \ne 1 \quad \text{or}\quad (a \ne 1 \quad \& \quad b \ne 1).
$$

Provide a practical interpretation of the above test result. [*Hint: $H_a$ basically says that there is no constraints for $a$ and $b$. Please review the lecture note for module 11  on the likelihood ratio test before coding.*]

\

```{r}

mle.result <- optim(
  par     = c(a = a_init, b = b_init),
  fn      = kuma.loglik,
  gr      = kuma.score,
  data    = reservoir,
  method  = "L-BFGS-B",
  lower   = c(1e-6, 1e-6),
  hessian = TRUE,
  control = list(fnscale = -1, maxit = 1000, abstol = 1e-8, trace = FALSE)
)

a_hat           <- mle.result$par["a"]
b_hat           <- mle.result$par["b"]
ll_unrestricted <- mle.result$value      # l(a_hat, b_hat)



# Restricted log-likelihood under H0: a = 1, b = 1

ll_null <- kuma.loglik(c(1, 1), reservoir)


# LRT statistic
# LR = -2 * [l(a_hat, b_hat) - l(1, 1)] -> chi^2(df = 2)


LR <- -2 * (ll_null - ll_unrestricted)   # equivalent to 2*(ll_unrestricted - ll_null)

cat("\n Likelihood Ratio Test \n",
"H0: a = 1 AND b = 1 (Uniform distribution)\n",
"Ha: a Does not equal 1 OR b Does not equal 1 (Full Kumaraswamy)\n",
"LR statistic:", LR, "\n")

# p-value (right-tailed chi-square, df = 2)

p.val <- 1 - pchisq(LR, df = 2)
cat("p-value:", p.val, "\n")


```
Reject H0, data does not follow a uniform distribution. 

A uniform distribution would imply that every storage level â€” from nearly empty to nearly full â€” is equally likely during the dry season. Rejecting means H0 means that storage levels are not uniformly distributed.
